scenes.title: What Problem Does MCP Solve?
scenes.narration: Before MCP, every AI application had to write custom glue code for every external tool or data source. That's the M-by-N integration problem: M apps times N tools means M-times-N adapters. MCP replaces that with a single JSON-RPC protocol. Each external capability runs as an MCP Server. Each AI app speaks MCP as a Client. One protocol, many integrations.
bullets: M×N adapters → M+N with one protocol
Client connects from the Host app

scenes.title: Three Roles: Host, Client, Server
scenes.narration: MCP has three roles. The Host is the AI application—in Tact, that's the Agent plus tact-ui. The Client is one connection inside the Host; one Client per Server. The Server is a separate process that exposes tools, resources, and prompts. The pattern is one Host, N Clients, N Servers.

scenes.title: Handshake: initialize
scenes.narration: After spawning a Server over stdio, the Client must send initialize before any tool calls. Both sides exchange protocol version and capabilities—what features they support, like tools with list-changed notifications. The Client then sends notifications slash initialized. In Tact, rmcp handles this inside serve; you won't find raw JSON in application code.

scenes.title: Tool Discovery and Naming
scenes.narration: Next, the Client calls tools slash list. Each tool has a name, description, and input schema. Tact prefixes MCP tools as mcp double-underscore plugin double-underscore server double-underscore tool—so multiple servers never collide. Native tools and MCP tools merge into one list the LLM sees every turn.

scenes.title: Execution: tools/call
scenes.narration: When the LLM picks a tool, the Agent routes names starting with mcp double-underscore to MCPToolRouter. The router parses the server and tool name, then sends tools slash call with the Server-internal tool name—not the prefixed Agent name. Results come back as a content array; Tact joins text parts and feeds them back into the conversation.

scenes.title: Dynamic Updates and Shutdown Gaps
scenes.narration: The MCP protocol supports notifications slash tools slash list changed when a server's tool set changes. Tact currently fetches tools once at startup; live tool-list refresh is not wired yet. On exit, tact-ui calls Agent::shutdown_mcp to disconnect_all MCP child processes.
